coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis of Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites and their application for electrochemical sensing of hydroquinone
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Tingting
Xu, Jinqiong
Arsalan, Muhammad
Sheng, Qinglin
Zheng, Jianbin
Cao, Wei
Yue, Tianli
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a facile and effective method for the synthesis of Au@Pd nanocomposites via a chemical reduction method was proposed. Moreover, a novel electrochemical sensor based on the Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites were constructed and used for detection of hydroquinone (HQ). The morphologies and structures of Au nanorods (NRs) and Au@Pd nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and electrochemical methods. By tuning the amount of Pd precursor, different Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites were gained. It could be clearly shown that the Au3@Pd6 (nAu:nPd = 3:6) modified the glassy carbon electrode (Au3@Pd6/GCE) possessed a sensitive electrochemical response toward HQ and the oxidation peak appeared at nearly 0 V at the potential window range of −0.3 to 0.3 V. Meanwhile, the experimental results found a broad concentration range of 4–5000 µM, a low detection limit of 0.63 µM and desirable sensitivity of 1.127 mA mM−1 cm−2. The mentioned sensor was successfully applied for HQ determination in the lake water and tap water samples. It is expected that the proposed method can be used for the synthesis of other metal nanostructures with distinguished electrocatalytic abilities and used for environmental monitoring of hydroquinone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au@Pd
Core-shell nanocomposites
Electrochemical sensor
Hydroquinone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nanocomposites and preparation of sensor
Electrochemical behavior of HQ on different modified electrodes
Reproducibility and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) Au NRs, (B) Au3@Pd2, (C) Au3@Pd4, (D) Au3@Pd6 and (E and F) Au3@Pd8. HRTEM images of Au NRs (inset of A) and Au3@Pd6 (inset of C).
UV–vis spectroscopy of Au NRs, Au3@Pd2, Au3@Pd4, Au3@Pd6 and Au3@Pd8.
CV curves of the bare GCE, the Au/GCE, the Au3@Pd2/GCE, the Au3@Pd4/GCE, the Au3@Pd6/GCE and the Au3@Pd8/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 500 µM HQ with scan rate 50 mV s−1.

(A) CV curves of 500 µM HQ at the Au3@Pd6/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) with different scan rate(10–125 mV s−1). (B) the peak currents versus the square root of scan rates for HQ. (C) the peak potentials versus the logarithm value of scan rate for HQ.

(A) DPV curves of 500 µM HQ at the Pd3@Au6/GCE in 0.1 M PBS with different pH (pH 5.0–8.5). (B) the peak currents versus pH for HQ (red line) and the peak potentials versus pH for HQ (black line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) DPV curves of the Au3@Pd6/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in different concentration of HQ (4–5000 µM). (B) the corresponding concentration of HQ versus the peak currents.
DPV curves of the Au3@Pd6/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.5 mM HQ, 1.0 mM CC, 1.0 mM RC and 1.0 mM p-nitrophenol, respectively.
Schematic diagram of the synthetic process of different Au@Pd nanocomposites and schematic diagram for the detection of HQ using Au3@Pd6/GCE.
Comparison of HQ detection among different modified electrodes.
Detection of HQ by Au3@Pd6/GCE in real samples.

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites and their application for electrochemical sensing of hydroquinone

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Materials Science/Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Nanjing University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a facile and effective method for the synthesis of Au@Pd nanocomposites via a chemical reduction method was proposed. Moreover, a novel electrochemical sensor based on the Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites were constructed and used for detection of hydroquinone (HQ). The morphologies and structures of Au nanorods (NRs) and Au@Pd nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and electrochemical methods. By tuning the amount of Pd precursor, different Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites were gained. It could be clearly shown that the Au3@Pd6 (nAu:nPd = 3:6) modified the glassy carbon electrode (Au3@Pd6/GCE) possessed a sensitive electrochemical response toward HQ and the oxidation peak appeared at nearly 0 V at the potential window range of −0.3 to 0.3 V. Meanwhile, the experimental results found a broad concentration range of 4–5000 µM, a low detection limit of 0.63 µM and desirable sensitivity of 1.127 mA mM−1 cm−2. The mentioned sensor was successfully applied for HQ determination in the lake water and tap water samples. It is expected that the proposed method can be used for the synthesis of other metal nanostructures with distinguished electrocatalytic abilities and used for environmental monitoring of hydroquinone.
Scheme 1 Schematic diagram of the synthetic process of different Au@Pd nanocomposites and schematic diagram for the detection of HQ using Au3@Pd6/GCE.

By controlling the amount of palladium precursor, we successfully synthesize different mole ratio of Au@Pd core-shell nanocomposites including Au3@Pd2/GCE, Au3@Pd4/GCE, Au3@Pd6/GCE and Au3@Pd8/GCE. When the mole ratio of Au/Pd is 3:6, the Au3@Pd6 modified the glassy carbon electrode (Au3@Pd6/GCE) possess the optimal performance. So the Au3@Pd6/GCE was chosen as the work electrode to detect HQ, as shown in Scheme 1.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic compounds are a broad class of organic compounds and are used as byproducts in various industries. In particular, HQ (1,4-dihydroxybenzene) is one of isomers of dihydroxybenzene that is important in various biological processes. This compound has significant biological and environmental importance like isoenzyme inhibitory, antioxidation, antivirus, and antifungal activity [1,2]. Furthermore, as one of the critical raw materials, HQ is extensively used in the manufacture of cosmetics, paper, dye, photographic developers, pesticides, coal tar production, and pharmaceutical industries [3,4]. During manufacturing production processes, HQ can be continuously released into the water and soil and then absorbed by organisms in the environment, which brings numerous severe threats to people's health and life, such as the nausea, fatigue, tinnitus, headache, muscle twitching, collapse, dyspnea, nephropathy, and liver damage [5]. Moreover, the American Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European Union (EU) announce that priority pollutants include HQ owing to its bad biodegradable ability and high toxicity in the environment [6]. Therefore, a greatly selective and sensitive analytical methodology for the determination of HQ is urgently needed.

In recent years, diverse analytical techniques of detecting HQ have been widely reported, including fluorescence [7,8], spectrophotometry [9,10], capillary electrophoresis [11], electrochemiluminescence [12], photoluminescence [13], high-performance liquid chromatography [14], and colorimetric analysis [15]. However, these traditional analytical techniques have several limitations such as require complex manipulations, time-consuming sample pretreatment steps, complicated analysis procedures, and strict safe operation rules. Compared to these methods, electrochemical analytical method can achieve high sensitivity, good reproducibility, low toxicity, high energy density, fast response and real-time analysis [16–18]. It has attracted significant interest, which is exploited to detect and quantify different materials in diverse fields. Velmurugan et al. used activated graphene oxide to modify screen printed carbon electrode for studying the electrochemical oxidative behaviors of HQ and CC [19]. Wang et al. successfully prepared Co3O4-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots nanocomposite for the electrochemical analysis of HQ [20].

Unfortunately, the conventional bare electrodes are not suitable to detect HQ owing to the poor electrochemical response during the electrochemical detection process. Therefore, it is necessary to use the suitable multi-functional nanomaterials to modify bare electrodes for improving electron transfer rates. Noble metals have been extensively employed to modify electrodes in the electrochemical detection process due to its exceptional electrocatalytic properties [21]. Meanwhile, compared with monometallic materials, bimetallic nanocrystals display superior catalytic, electronic, and optical advantages [22]. In particular, core-shell structural bimetallic nanocrystals have unique advantages of higher catalytic performance and excellent stability [23,24]. Jiang and Du had successfully fabricated Au@Pd-RGO through one-step synthesis method and further used this sensor for the quantitative determination of dopamine, uric acid, and ascorbic acid, which showed Au@Pd compounds possessed the high catalytic activity [25]. A TNT sensor based on depositing PtPd concave nanocubes on graphene nanoribbons was designed by Zhang's group [26]. The electrochemical measurements display that the new prepared sensor can get higher current signals than bare GCE for TNT reduction.

In this work, we successfully synthesize a facile sensor of HQ based on GCE modified with Au@Pd core-shell nanomaterials. Au NRs are synthesized by the seed-mediated method, on which Pd nanoparticles (NPs) are loaded. Pd NPs are acted as shell, which can expand surface-to-volume ratio. These new nanocomposites possess high catalytic activity and selectivity in the process of determining HQ. We believe that the fabricated HQ sensor can have potential applications in pharmaceutical analysis, clinical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O, >99.9%) and palladium chloride (PdCl2, >99%) were purchased from Shanghai Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9%) were got from Guangdong Guanghua Chemical Factory Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CC), resorcinol (RC) and p-nitrophenol were sourced from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), L-ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), glucose and cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) were got from Sigma-Aldrich.

To prepare H2PdCl4 solution (0.056 M), 1 g of PdCl2 powder was dissolved by dropping 500 µL of 5% HCl solution under ultrasonication, then transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask, finally mixed for the subsequent experiments. All glassware used in the following experiments were put into ‘‘aqua regia’’ fresh solution (3:1 HCl:HNO3) for three hours and were cleaned thoroughly with double distilled water (DDW), then were dried in a vacuum desiccator.

UV–visible spectra were taken on a T6 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Persee, Beijing, China). The TEM images and HRTEM images were carried out using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI, USA). The electrochemical measurements were carried out using an IGS1130 electrochemical workstation (Guangzhou Insens Sensor Technology Co. Ltd, China) with a traditional three-electrode cell. The working electrodes were the bare GCE of 3 mm diameter and Au@Pd/GCE. The reference electrode and counter electrode respectively were a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum wire.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanocomposites and preparation of sensor

ce:section.ce:para: Using the seed-mediated method small isotropic Au NPs (about 4 nm) were synthesized [27]. Typically, HAuCl4·4H2O (300 µL, 10 mM) was added into CTAB (10 mL, 0.1 M) solution at 30 °C to form a light yellow solution. After that, an ice-cold freshly NaBH4 (0.8 mL, 0.01 M) solution was rapidly added into the resulting mixture under vigorous stirring. Immediately, the solution color changed into dark brown, indicating that nuclear gold had been formed. After quick stirring for 3 min, the obtained solution was kept at about 25 °C for 3.5 h for further experiment.

For the preparation of growth solution containing Au NRs, 1.09 g CTAB was dissolved in 30 mL of DDW to obtain 0.1 M CTAB solution. Next, HAuCl4·4H2O solution (1.8 mL, 10 mM) was added into the above solution in water bath at 40 °C. Sequentially the freshly prepared 0.288 mL of 0.10 M AA was quickly injected under quick magnetic stirring. At this point, the color of the reaction solution instantly turned into transparent. Finally, adding the obtained seed solution to the above mixture with gently stirring about 2 min, the color of the mixture changed into violet. The obtained solution was maintained at 40 °C for 10 h and centrifuged (10 min, 10,000 rpm) to remove excess impurities. Then the obtained Au NRs were washed two times with DDW and were re-dispersed in 6 mL of DDW for subsequent Au@Pd synthesis.

Jing and Wang's work was used as the reference for the preparation of Au@Pd [28]. There are four 10 mL centrifuge tubes. In every tube, 0.028 g CTAC was dissolved in 3.5 mL of DDW and the 250 µL yielded growth solution was introduced. 50 µL, 100 µL, 150 µL and 200 µL of 10 mM H2PdCl4 and 25 µL, 50 µL, 75 µL and 100 µL of 0.1 M AA was separately injected into four centrifuge tubes and thoroughly mixed. The resulting mixture was kept under static conditions for 4.5 h at 35 °C. According to Au/Pd precursors mole ratio, these products were called Au3@Pd2, Au3@Pd4, Au3@Pd6 and Au3@Pd8, respectively. Then specific synthetic procedure of different Au@Pd nanocomposites is shown ( Scheme 1).

GCE was firstly polished with 0.3 µm and 0.05 µm alumina slurry, then ultrasonically cleaned in DDW, followed by drying in N2 stream. The different Au@Pd suspensions and the remaining Au NRs suspension were separately centrifuged (10 min, 10,000 rpm), subsequently washed with DDW twice to obtain the corresponding clean precipitates, and then dried in a vacuum oven (30 °C, 5 h). 0.25 mg each solid were dispersed in 250 µL chitosan (CS, 0.05%). Herein, the materials containing appropriate concentration of CS can form a stable film on the electrode surface because of the good adhesive property of CS [29]. 7 µL of each obtained mixture was dropped on the surface of GCE to form diverse work electrodes including Au3@Pd2/GCE, Au3@Pd4/GCE, Au3@Pd6/GCE, Au3@Pd8/GCE, and Au/GCE. Au3@Pd6/GCE is taken as an example to illustrate the schematic diagram for the HQ detection (Scheme 1).

TEM and HRTEM were employed to investigate the structures and morphologies of Au NRs and different Au@Pd nanocomposites. Subsequently, ImageJ software was used for the size measurements of obtained images. Fig. 1A shows the low magnification TEM images, which reveal that the smooth-faced Au NRs are uniformly dispersed and the both ends are the same size. The nanorod's length is approximately 47 nm and the aspect ratio is about 5. The HRTEM image of Au NRs indicates the nanorod is the single-crystalline structure with face center cubic of {100} face (Fig. 1A inset). As seen from Fig. 1B–E, the morphology of materials change when Pd NPs are immobilized on the Au NRs substrates. Additionally, the Pd shell thickness and Pd NPs size increase with the rising of palladium content. However, when the mole ratio of Au and Pd precursors is 3:8, it is clearly observed that the part of Pd NPs are excessively aggregated on the Au NRs and another part of Pd NPs deposit on itself core to form spherical materials without formation of bimetal compounds (Fig. 1E and F). The HRTEM image of palladium nanoparticle illustrates that palladium is single crystalline with lattice planes of {200} in the inset of Fig. 1D, which presents that Pd NPs are successfully synthesized on the Au NRs.

Fig. 2 presents that the UV–vis spectroscopy of different Au @ Pd nanocomposites and Au NRs. It is observed that the absorbance of Au NRs at 920.4 nm is almost 3.5-fold higher than that of Au NRs at 510.5 nm, indicating that the uniformly distributed Au NRs are successfully synthesized. Because the dipolar plasmon oscillations of Au NRs were inhibited by Pd NPs, the UV–vis spectroscopy of the Au NRs will disappear when Pd NPs coat the Au NRs [30]. By increasing the content of palladium precursor, the characteristic peak at 291.5 nm obviously enhances, which manifests that Pd shell on the surface of Au NRs gradually thickens [31]. It is notable that the UV–vis absorption spectra of Au3@Pd6 significantly enhances, probably because of the saturation of palladium content on Au NRs by comparison of Au3@Pd2 and the Au3@Pd4. However, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Au3@Pd8 just slightly rise owing to the aggregation of Pd NPs without well-defined core-shell structural composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of HQ on different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a fast and effective way to assess the performance of different modified electrodes [32]. Hence, CV method was used to investigate the electrochemical behavior of various electrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 500 µM, as presented in Fig. 3. It is also well known gold nanomaterials possess good catalytic and electrical conductive activity [33,34]. By attaching Au NRs on the electrode surface, the Au/GCE exhibits higher peak current than bare electrode. However, the single metal is easily aggregated and is lack of stability, which may result in the bad morphology and poor performance. To improve the performance, the Pd NPs were deposited on Au NRs to fabricate a core-shell bimetal Au@Pd by using ascorbic acid to reduce H2PdCl4. Core-shell bimetal can enhance catalytic properties and stability because of its excellent structure and synergistic effects [35]. Especially, palladium possesses outstanding electrical conductivity and Pd NPs on the surface of Au NRs provides higher surface [36,37]. With the content of palladium precursor increasing, the redox peak currents clearly increased. When the molar ratio of Au to Pd is set as 1:2, the Au3@Pd6/GCE presents the highest electrocatalytic current relative to other modified electrodes. The oxidation peak current of the Au3@Pd6/GCE (23.220 µA) is 2.16 times greater than that of the bare GCE (10.767 µA). Nevertheless, when the molar ratio of Au/Pd is 3:8, the peak current of Au3@Pd8/GCE declined. This can be attributed that the active surface area has reached the point of saturation when the molar of Au to Pd ratio is 1:2 and the excessive deposition of Pd NPs lead to the disappearance of the characteristics of Au NRs. Hence, the Au3@Pd6/GCE was chosen as the work electrode for the next experiments.

The influence of scan rate was investigated by using Au3@Pd6/GCE to detect HQ by CV ( Fig. 4A). As the scan rate increases (10–150 mV s−1), the redox peak currents of HQ continuously rise and the peak potentials evidently shift. Fig. 4B shows that both the oxidation peak currents (I
pa ) and the reduction peak currents (I
pc ) of HQ separately have significant linear relationships with the square root of the scan rates, I
pa (µA) = 4.568 + 2.643 ν 1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R 2 = 0.996) and I
pc (µA) = 6.763–3.323 ν 1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R 2 = 0.9961). The electrochemical analysis process of HQ by Au3@Pd6/GCE is identified as a special diffusion-controlled process [38–40]. The electrochemical reaction kinetic parameter of HQ is studied in Fig. 4C. The anodic peak potentials (E
pa ) and the cathodic peak potentials (E
pc ) of HQ are respectively linearly related to the logarithm value of scan rate (log (ν)), E
pa (mV) = 0.040 + 0.055 ν 1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R 2 = 0.999) and E
pa (mV) = 0.0266–0.033 ν 1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R 2 = 0.998). The phenomenon shows the electrode reaction is a quasi-reversible process [41].

Hydroquinone is a weak acid because its molecule contains hydroxyl-groups. The protons in solution can affect the electrode reaction [20]. In order to further test the electrochemical behavior of HQ, (DPV) curves of 500 µM HQ were measured at the Au3@Pd6/GCE in different pH solution ( Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B (red line) shows the oxidation peak current of HQ at Au3 @Pd6/GCE in different pH ranges of 5.0–8.5. With pH rising from 5 to 7.0, the anodic peak currents of HQ increase. However, the peak currents gradually decrease when the pH grow from 7.0 to 8.5. The maximum peak current is obtained at pH 7.0 for HQ, so pH 7.0 is chosen as the best pH for the subsequent experiments to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity. Fig. 5B (black line) presents that the oxidation peak potentials of HQ are linear to different pH values at Au3 @Pd6/GCE. With increasing pH, the anodic peak potentials of HQ have an obvious shift toward negative direction, suggesting that the protons actually take part in the electrochemical reaction [42,43]. The regression equation for HQ is expressed as E
pa (mV) = 0.415–0.062 pH (R 2 = 0.995). It can be found that the shift of 61.8 mV every pH unit, which approximately equals to the theoretical value of 58.5 mV every pH unit. Base on the Nernst Equation (dE
p /dpH = 2.303 mRT/nF), the electrochemical process of HQ at Au3@Pd6/GCE involves with two electrons and two protons transfer [44] and the corresponding formula is as follows.

Under the optimum experiment conditions, the Au3@Pd6/GCE was employed to detect HQ. Fig. 6A shows the anodic peak currents of HQ gradually goes up with increasing concentration. In the low and high concentration, the anodic peak currents of HQ were different linearly correlated with the concentration (Fig. 6B). The one equation of linear regression is I
pa (µA) = 1.496 + 0.079 c (µM) (R 2 = 0.998) in the concentration range of 4–1000 µM HQ. The other regression equation is I
pa (µA) = 33.330 + 0.047 c (µM) (R 2 = 0.997) in the concentration range of 1000–5000 µM HQ. It is revealed that the sensor possesses the low detection limit of 0.63 µM (S/N = 3) and superior sensitivity of 1.127 mA mM−1 cm−2. The electrode is compared with other different materials modified electrodes and the obtained result is listed in Table 1. By comparing with the other electrodes, the Au3@Pd6/GCE possessed a border linear range and a lower detection limit. Hence, the Au3@Pd6/GCE sensor is highly suitable to detect HQ directly.

The potential influences of inorganic and organic matters for the detection of HQ were estimated. These substances that may appear in lake water were selected as the interferents to complete this study, which included 2-times concentration of phenolic compounds (CC, RC, p-nitrophenol) and 5-times concentration of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid, glucose and 100-times concentration of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −. Fig. 7 depicts the anodic peaks of HQ, CC, RC and p-nitrophenol are −0.01 V, 0.165 V, 0.41 V and 0.753 V, respectively, demonstrating that these phenolic compounds appear characteristic peaks at specific voltages. The peak current of 0.5 mM HQ is higher than that of 1.0 mM other phenolic compounds, in particular, which is about 3 times higher than that of 1.0 mM RC and 1.4 times higher than that of 1.0 mM p-nitrophenol. With the exception of phenolic compounds, the other organic and inorganic matters are evaluated in the presence of 0.5 mM HQ (pH 7.0). It is found that the interference of the substances is largely ineffective because the variation in peak current is less than 5%. These results manifest that the fabricated Au3@Pd6/GCE possesses the ability of anti-interference in the experiment of quantifying HQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Five electrodes, which were independently modified with the same content of Au3@Pd6 core-shell bimetal composites, were tested by DPV under the HQ concentration of 0.5 mM to estimate the reproducibility of the present sensor. It suggested that the electrochemical responses of these electrodes are rather similar and the relative standard deviations (RSD) (<3%) are also acceptable, which exhibits the Au3@Pd6/GCE possessed excellent reproducibility. Besides, one of above five electrodes was used in subsequent experiments for detecting and assessing stability of Au3@Pd6/GCE. Whereafter, the fabricated electrode was kept undisturbedly at about 25 °C temperature for a month, the peak current of HQ reduced 2.8% of the initial current value. These results suggest that Au3@Pd6/GCE holds long-term ability. Therefore, considering the good repeatability and durability of Au3@Pd6/GCE, the designed electrode can be explored to analyze HQ.

The tap water and the lake water were served as actual samples to assess the practical application of Au3@Pd6/GCE in the process of detecting HQ. The HQ went undetected in the real samples. The reason may be the concentration of HQ in the samples below the detection limit. The standard addition method has been proved to be an effective and reliable method for the trace analysis. Considering the very low level of the target analytes in the sample, the known concentration of HQ was added into a certain volume of sample. After that, the amounts of HQ were measured by DPV technique and were analyzed by the standard addition method, which is illustrated in Table 2. The scope of recovery is 97.92–104.25% and the RSD of detection is below 3.8%, which manifests the as-made electrochemical sensor of Au3@Pd6/GCE can accurately and precisely determine trace amounts of HQ in real samples.

In summary, Au@Pd core-shell bimetallic nanocomposites containing the various thicknesses of Pd shell were successfully synthesized by controlling the mole ratio of Au/Pd, after which the nanocomposites were applied to establish an electrochemical HQ sensor. The Au3@Pd6/GCE was used to detect HQ with long-term stability, satisfactory reproducibility and high selectivity, resulting from superior catalytic performance and synergetic effect of Au@Pd bimetal. These results show the Au3@Pd6/GCE is reliable for the detection of HQ in the lake water and tap water. Furthermore, the new sensor could have potential practical applications of HQ sensing in pharmaceutical analysis, clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this project by the National Science Fund of China (21575113), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS1811), and the Natural Science Fund of Shaanxi Province in China (2017JM2036).

ce:section.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A glassy carbon electrode modified with poly(2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine) for simultaneous detection of dihydroxybenzene isomers

Conjugated polymer nanoparticles-based fluorescent biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of hydroquinone
Tailor-made dicationic ionic liquid as a fluorescent sensor for detection of hydroquinone and catechol
Rauf, Ultra-trace level determination of hydroquinone in waste photographic solutions by UV–vis spectrophotometry
Construction of Au nanoparticles on choline chloride modified glassy carbon electrode for sensitive detection of nitrite
Application of thermally reduced graphene oxide modified electrode in simultaneous determination of dihydroxybenzene isomers
Sensitive detection of hydroquinone based on electrochemiluminescence energy transfer between the exited ZnSe quantum dots and benzoquinone

Fabrication of CeVO4, as nanozyme for facile colorimetric discrimination of hydroquinone from resorcinol and catechol

ynergic effect of silver nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes on the simultaneous voltammetric determination of hydroquinone, catechol, bisphenol A and phenol

Electrochemical preparation of activated graphene oxide for the simultaneous determination of hydroquinone and catechol
Nanohybrid of Co3O4 and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydroquinone
